modelsAndApis.useCase: Ingredient conflict detection and layering order suggestions
modelsAndApis.rationale: Claude 4.5 Sonnet excels at structured reasoning over ingredient lists. When a user adds products to a routine, the ingredient arrays are passed as structured JSON to Claude with a curated prompt containing known conflict rules (Retinol+AHA, Vitamin C+Niacinamide nuance, etc.). Claude returns plain-language conflict warnings and an ordered application sequence. Sonnet is chosen over Opus for cost efficiency — this is a high-frequency, latency-sensitive call that fires on every routine edit. Response is cached by ingredient fingerprint hash to avoid redundant API calls.

modelsAndApis.useCase: Skin Story correlation engine — pattern analysis and insight generation
modelsAndApis.rationale: Claude 4.5 Sonnet is used to synthesize weekly and monthly correlation summaries from pre-aggregated structured data (skin condition scores, routine completion rates, product usage frequency, UV index averages). The model does not receive raw photos or facial data — only numerical and categorical logged data. Sonnet's context window handles 90-day log histories comfortably. Insight generation runs as a scheduled background job (weekly), not on-demand, keeping latency irrelevant and cost predictable.

modelsAndApis.useCase: Product identification and ingredient list extraction from barcode scan or photo of product label
modelsAndApis.rationale: GPT-5.4 with vision is used when a barcode lookup fails to return a match in the local database or Open Food Facts / INCI-style APIs. The user can photograph the product label and GPT-5.4 Vision extracts product name, brand, and full ingredient list (INCI format) from the image. This is a fallback path only — not the primary flow — so higher per-call cost is acceptable. Vision accuracy on ingredient label text is significantly better than OCR-only approaches, especially for Korean and Japanese label fonts.

modelsAndApis.useCase: Beginner ingredient education — adaptive plain-language explanations
modelsAndApis.rationale: When a user taps an ingredient to learn more, Claude 4.5 Sonnet generates a plain-language explanation calibrated to the user's self-reported experience level (beginner, intermediate, enthusiast). The prompt includes the user's experience tier and the ingredient name. Responses are cached aggressively by ingredient+tier combination — the vast majority of queries will hit cache after the first few thousand users, making marginal cost near zero at scale.

modelsAndApis.useCase: Active ingredient scheduling safety analysis — conflict detection across scheduled days
modelsAndApis.rationale: When a user schedules routines containing actives (retinol, AHAs, BHAs, Vitamin C, benzoyl peroxide), Claude 4.5 Sonnet analyzes the full weekly schedule to flag dangerous consecutive-day combinations (e.g., retinol Monday night + AHA Tuesday morning). Input is a structured JSON schedule object, not free text. This runs server-side on schedule save, not in real-time, so latency is acceptable. Sonnet is sufficient — Opus is not needed for this structured reasoning task.

modelsAndApis.useCase: Community-submitted ingredient data moderation and error flagging
modelsAndApis.rationale: When users submit new products with manually entered ingredient lists, Claude 4.5 Sonnet performs a lightweight validation pass — checking for obviously malformed INCI names, suspicious duplicates, and likely OCR errors — before the submission enters the moderation queue. This reduces human moderator workload by pre-filtering low-quality submissions. Runs as an async background job via Inngest, so latency is irrelevant. Cost is minimal given low submission volume relative to reads.

dataNeeded: User-logged skin condition entries (breakout severity, dryness, redness, sensitivity — numeric scale 1–5, user-defined)
Products owned by user (name, brand, INCI ingredient list, product type/category, pH if known)
Routine definitions (product sequence, scheduled days/intervals, AM/PM designation, timezone)
Routine completion logs (timestamp, which products applied, any skipped products)
Progress photos (optional, user-initiated, stored as user-owned assets)
Environmental data (UV index, humidity, AQI — fetched from open weather APIs by user location, not stored as PII — stored as daily aggregate values keyed to date only)
User experience level (beginner/intermediate/enthusiast — self-reported at onboarding, editable)
Skin type and primary concerns (self-reported, optional, used only for layering suggestions)
Barcode scan history (product barcodes scanned, used to build community database — anonymized)
Community product submissions (product name, brand, ingredient list, submitter pseudonym — moderated before publication)
dataStrategy.collectionApproach: All personal skin data is collected exclusively through explicit user action — no passive data collection, no background sensor access, no facial scanning. Environmental data (UV index, humidity) is fetched on-demand from the Open-Meteo API (open source, no API key required for moderate usage) using coarse location (city-level, not GPS coordinates) that the user explicitly grants via Expo Location with a clear permission prompt explaining exactly why location is needed. Location is never stored server-side — only the resulting daily environmental aggregate values are persisted, keyed to a date string with no geographic identifier beyond timezone. Progress photos are stored in Supabase Storage in a user-scoped private bucket — never processed by any AI model unless the user explicitly triggers label scanning. Product data submitted by users is pseudonymous (linked to an internal user ID, never email or name) and enters a moderation queue before becoming public. All AI inference calls to Claude and GPT-5.4 Vision are made server-side via Supabase Edge Functions — raw ingredient lists and skin logs never leave the server to a third-party API with user-identifying metadata attached. Prompts are constructed to strip any PII before dispatch.
dataStrategy.complianceNotes: GDPR (EU/UK): GlowLog collects special category data under GDPR Article 9 — health-related data including skin conditions. Legal basis is explicit consent obtained at onboarding with a clear, plain-language consent screen (not buried in ToS). Users have the right to export all their data (JSON export is a free-tier feature, not paywalled) and the right to deletion — a single 'Delete My Account' button triggers a cascading delete across Supabase PostgreSQL, Supabase Storage, and any cached AI outputs. A Data Processing Agreement (DPA) must be executed with Anthropic (Claude API) and OpenAI (GPT-5.4 Vision) before launch, as skin condition data may be included in prompts. CCPA (California): Users are not sold to advertisers — GlowLog's revenue model is subscription-only. A 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link is included in settings as required, though no selling occurs. COPPA: App is rated 17+ on App Store and 18+ on Google Play to avoid COPPA obligations — skincare actives context makes this appropriate. South Korea PIPA: Given the K-beauty target audience, data localization is not required for apps without a Korean legal entity, but privacy policy must be available in Korean and consent flows must meet PIPA standards for sensitive personal information. Progress photos are classified as biometric-adjacent and require separate explicit consent before the photo feature is enabled. All data is stored in Supabase's EU region (eu-west-1) by default, with a US region option for US-only users. Retention policy: skin logs and routine data retained indefinitely while account is active; deleted within 30 days of account deletion request.

architecture.processingModel: Hybrid: lightweight rule-based logic runs on-device for immediate feedback (ingredient conflict pre-screening using a bundled JSON conflict rules database of ~500 known pairs, routine scheduling validation, streak calculation), while all LLM inference runs server-side via Supabase Edge Functions. On-device processing uses a bundled ingredient conflict rules JSON (~200KB) compiled from open INCI databases, enabling instant conflict warnings without any network call for the most common ingredient pairs. LLM calls are reserved for nuanced analysis (full routine ingredient synthesis, Skin Story correlation summaries, label vision extraction) and are never made directly from the React Native client — all AI calls are proxied through Edge Functions to protect API keys and enable server-side PII stripping. react-native-fast-tflite is explicitly NOT used in v1 — the correlation engine is statistical (Pearson correlation on logged numeric data) rather than a trained ML model, keeping the app lightweight and avoiding the significant-setup overhead of on-device ML for an initial launch.
architecture.apiArchitecture: REST-based API layer built on Supabase Edge Functions (Deno runtime) serving the React Native client (Expo SDK 52, RN 0.76+). Each AI feature maps to a dedicated Edge Function endpoint: /functions/v1/analyze-ingredients (ingredient conflict + layering), /functions/v1/generate-skin-story (weekly correlation summary), /functions/v1/extract-product-label (GPT-5.4 Vision fallback), /functions/v1/explain-ingredient (beginner education), /functions/v1/validate-schedule (active ingredient scheduling safety). Background jobs (weekly Skin Story generation, community submission moderation) run via Inngest v3.x triggered by Supabase database webhooks — Inngest handles retries, rate limiting, and fan-out without blocking the client. Product search uses Algolia v4.x for the community product database (fast fuzzy search, typo tolerance critical for ingredient name search). Barcode lookup hits Open Food Facts REST API first, then falls back to the GPT-5.4 Vision label extraction path if no match is found. Supabase PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM handles all relational data. Supabase Auth handles authentication. RevenueCat SDK 8+ manages subscription state on the mobile client, with webhook events synced to Supabase to gate premium features server-side.
architecture.cachingStrategy: Three-tier caching strategy: (1) On-device bundle cache — the ingredient conflict rules JSON (~200KB) and a top-5000 product ingredient cache are bundled with the app and updated via Expo Updates OTA pushes, not app store releases. This enables instant conflict checking for common products with zero network latency. (2) Upstash Redis for server-side response caching — ingredient analysis responses are cached by a SHA-256 hash of the sorted ingredient fingerprint (ingredient names normalized to lowercase, sorted alphabetically) with a 30-day TTL. Beginner ingredient explanations are cached by ingredient_slug+experience_tier with a 90-day TTL. Cache hit rate is expected to exceed 80% within 60 days of launch as the most common ingredient combinations are covered. (3) Supabase PostgreSQL materialized views for Skin Story aggregates — pre-computed weekly correlation summaries are stored as JSONB in a skin_story_cache table, refreshed by the Inngest weekly job. The client reads from this cache table rather than triggering live LLM inference on every dashboard load. Cache invalidation: ingredient cache invalidated when a product's ingredient list is updated via community moderation. Skin Story cache invalidated when new log entries are added (debounced — only regenerates if 7+ new entries since last generation).
fallbackMechanisms: Ingredient conflict detection fallback: if the Claude API call fails or times out (>3s), the on-device bundled conflict rules JSON is used exclusively. The UI displays a banner: 'Using offline ingredient data — some nuanced conflicts may not be shown.' Core conflict warnings (Retinol+AHA, Retinol+Benzoyl Peroxide) are always available offline via the bundled rules.

Barcode scan fallback chain: (1) Scan barcode → query local bundled product cache; (2) if miss → query Open Food Facts API; (3) if miss → query Algolia community product database; (4) if miss → offer GPT-5.4 Vision label photo extraction; (5) if Vision API fails → offer fully manual entry form. No user is ever blocked — manual entry is always the final fallback.

Skin Story generation fallback: if the Inngest weekly job fails after 3 retries, the dashboard displays the last successfully generated Skin Story with a timestamp ('Last updated 8 days ago') rather than an error state. A simplified statistical summary (streak rate, most-used products, average skin score) is always computed client-side from raw log data as a baseline, ensuring the dashboard is never empty.

RevenueCat subscription state fallback: premium feature gates are checked against a local cached subscription state (stored in Supabase Auth user metadata, synced on app open) so that a RevenueCat API outage does not lock paying users out of premium features. Cache is considered valid for 48 hours before forcing a re-check.

Algolia search fallback: if Algolia is unreachable, product search falls back to a Supabase full-text search query (PostgreSQL tsvector on product name and brand fields). Results are less fuzzy but functional. A subtle UI indicator shows 'Search is in limited mode' without alarming the user.

costAnalysis.estimatedCostPerUser: $0.04–$0.09 per active user per month at steady state. Breakdown: Ingredient analysis (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) — assuming 15 routine edits/month per active user, 80% cache hit rate after Month 2, ~3 live API calls/month at ~$0.003 per call (avg 800 input tokens + 300 output tokens at Sonnet pricing) = ~$0.009/user/month. Skin Story generation (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) — 1 weekly summary per active user = 4 calls/month at ~$0.008 per call (avg 2000 input tokens + 500 output tokens) = ~$0.032/user/month. Ingredient education (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) — assuming 5 ingredient lookups/month, 90% cache hit after Month 3, ~0.5 live calls at $0.003 = ~$0.0015/user/month. GPT-5.4 Vision label extraction — fallback path only, estimated 10% of new product additions use Vision, ~2 new products/month per user = 0.2 Vision calls at ~$0.02/call = ~$0.004/user/month. Algolia search — free tier covers first 10K searches/month; at 1,500 MAU with 10 searches/user = 15K searches, estimated $0.005/user/month above free tier. Upstash Redis — negligible at this scale, within free tier for first 6 months. Supabase — Pro plan at $25/month covers up to ~5,000 MAU comfortably. Total blended: ~$0.05/user/month for free users. Premium users generate identical AI costs. At $7.99/month premium price and 3% conversion, the 97% free users cost ~$0.05 each and the 3% paying users generate $7.99 — the unit economics are strongly positive from the first paying user.
optimizationStrategies: Aggressive ingredient analysis caching via Upstash Redis keyed on ingredient fingerprint hash — target 80%+ cache hit rate by Month 2, reducing Claude API calls by 4x compared to uncached. This is the single highest-impact optimization given ingredient analysis is the most frequent AI call.

Batch Skin Story generation via Inngest weekly jobs rather than on-demand generation — process all users needing a weekly summary in a single scheduled job with controlled concurrency (max 10 parallel Claude calls), avoiding API rate limit spikes and enabling bulk pricing benefits.

On-device bundled conflict rules JSON for the top 500 most common ingredient conflict pairs — eliminates API calls entirely for the majority of conflict checks. Updated via Expo Updates OTA (not app store release) when new conflict data is added, keeping the bundle current without user action.

Tiered AI feature access: Skin Story correlation summaries (the most expensive AI feature at ~$0.032/user/month) are a premium-only feature. Free users receive a simplified statistical summary (streak rate, average skin score trend) computed entirely from database aggregates with no LLM call. This reduces AI costs for the 97% free user base by ~65%.

Model tier selection: use Claude 4.5 Sonnet (not Opus) for all production inference paths. Opus is reserved only for a potential future 'deep analysis' premium feature. Sonnet delivers sufficient quality for structured ingredient reasoning at approximately 5x lower cost than Opus.

Prompt compression: ingredient lists passed to Claude are normalized and deduplicated before dispatch — removing duplicate ingredients, standardizing INCI names, and truncating ingredient lists beyond position 30 (preserving the most relevant actives at the top of the INCI list). This reduces average input token count by ~30% with negligible impact on conflict detection accuracy.

risks.risk: Claude API hallucination on ingredient conflict advice — model confidently states an incorrect conflict or safe combination, leading a user to damage their skin barrier or miss a genuine risk
risks.mitigation: All ingredient conflict logic uses a two-layer approach: a deterministic rules engine (bundled JSON of verified conflict pairs sourced from peer-reviewed dermatology literature and INCIDecoder) runs first and produces authoritative binary conflict flags. Claude is used only for plain-language explanation and nuanced context — it cannot override the rules engine's conflict determination. UI copy always includes 'When in doubt, consult a dermatologist' and conflict warnings are labeled 'Based on known ingredient interactions' not 'Medical advice.' The rules JSON is versioned and reviewed by a skincare-knowledgeable advisor before each update.

risks.risk: GPT-5.4 Vision misreads ingredient labels — especially Korean/Japanese text — and populates incorrect ingredient lists that then drive flawed conflict analysis for that user
risks.mitigation: Vision-extracted ingredient lists are never silently accepted. After extraction, the user is shown the parsed ingredient list in an editable confirmation screen before it is saved. The UI explicitly states 'AI-extracted — please verify.' Extracted lists are flagged in the database as vision-sourced and excluded from community product submissions until a second user confirms the same product's ingredients independently. Confidence scoring from the Vision API response is used to trigger a lower-confidence warning banner when the model's output uncertainty is high.

risks.risk: Vendor lock-in to Anthropic Claude API — pricing changes, API deprecation, or service outages could disrupt core AI features with no migration path
risks.mitigation: All Claude API calls are abstracted behind an internal AIService interface in the Edge Functions layer. Switching to an alternative model (GPT-5.2 via openai-gpt5, or Google Gemini) requires only changing the model adapter, not the calling code. The bundled on-device conflict rules JSON ensures the most critical feature (ingredient conflict detection) continues functioning without any LLM dependency. Skin Story generation degrades gracefully to statistical summaries if the LLM is unavailable. No user-facing feature is 100% dependent on a single vendor's uptime.

risks.risk: AI inference latency degrades user experience — ingredient analysis taking 3–5 seconds on routine save creates a frustrating pause in a flow that should feel instant
risks.mitigation: Ingredient conflict checking uses the on-device bundled rules JSON for immediate feedback (< 50ms) shown as the user adds products. The Claude API call for nuanced explanation and layering order runs asynchronously in the background and updates the UI when complete, using an optimistic UI pattern — the user sees instant basic conflict flags and the detailed explanation loads in within 1–2 seconds without blocking interaction. Upstash Redis cache ensures repeat queries (same ingredient combination) return in < 100ms. A 3-second timeout on all Claude calls falls back to the on-device rules result with a 'Detailed analysis unavailable' note.

risks.risk: Environmental data collection (location for UV index) creates user privacy anxiety and permission denial rates that undermine the Skin Story correlation feature
risks.mitigation: Location permission is requested only when the user explicitly enables 'Environmental Tracking' in settings — it is off by default and never requested during onboarding. The permission prompt explains exactly what is collected ('Your city-level location is used to fetch today's UV index and humidity — it is never stored or shared'). If permission is denied, the environmental correlation feature is gracefully hidden rather than showing an error. Users can manually input their city name as an alternative, which is used for weather API lookup without any device location access. This opt-in design also reduces GDPR consent complexity.

risks.risk: Algolia search costs scale unexpectedly as the product database grows and search volume increases beyond the free tier, creating an unbudgeted infrastructure cost spike
risks.mitigation: Algolia usage is monitored via PostHog event tracking on search calls, with a Slack alert configured when monthly search volume exceeds 8,000 (80% of free tier threshold). If costs become significant, the fallback is Meilisearch v1.x self-hosted on a $6/month VPS — the Algolia client interface is abstracted behind a SearchService layer in Edge Functions, making the swap a configuration change rather than a rewrite. Meilisearch is evaluated as the primary search solution for v2 if Algolia costs exceed $30/month.

risks.risk: Skin Story correlation engine surfaces spurious correlations — telling a user that Product X is causing breakouts when the correlation is coincidental — eroding trust in the app's core value proposition
risks.mitigation: Correlation insights are only surfaced when statistical thresholds are met: minimum 14 data points for any correlation claim, minimum 0.4 Pearson correlation coefficient, and a minimum 30-day logging history before any pattern insights are shown. The UI language is explicitly probabilistic — 'Your skin tends to score lower on days following [Product X] use — this may be worth exploring' rather than '[Product X] is causing your breakouts.' A 'How is this calculated?' explainer is one tap away on every insight card. Insights are labeled 'Based on your last 30 days of logs' with the data point count shown, giving users the context to evaluate the claim themselves.
